headline: Iran reopened and re-closed the Strait of Hormuz within 24 hours, complicating US ceasefire talks as Israel-Lebanon truce faces violation claims.

sections.label: Situation
sections.title: Iran closes Strait again as diplomatic momentum stalls
paragraphs: Iran announced it would reopen the strategic Strait of Hormuz on Saturday after briefly relaxing restrictions, but reversed course within hours and reimposed the closure. Iran cited 'repeated breaches of trust' as the US maintained its naval blockade on Iranian ports, with at least nine vessels passing through during the brief reopening period.

Iran's Supreme National Security Council said Tehran would maintain 'supervision and control over transit through the Strait of Hormuz until the definitive end of the war', citing the need to cut off American military supply routes. The US has forced 23 vessels to turn around since beginning its blockade on April 13.
key_facts: Iran closed Strait of Hormuz again after brief Saturday reopening
Nine vessels passed through during temporary relaxation of restrictions
US naval blockade has turned away 23 ships since April 13
Iran demands control over strait 'until definitive end of war'
sections.australia_relevance: Australia heavily depends on oil refined in Southeast Asian countries that import crude through the Strait of Hormuz, making the closure critical for fuel supplies.

sections.label: Background
sections.title: 50-day conflict sparked by Iranian leader's assassination
paragraphs: The current conflict began after Israel assassinated Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei in an air attack on February 28, with Iran confirming his death on March 1. The US joined Israeli strikes against Iranian military targets, with Iran retaliating by launching hundreds of drones and missiles at Israel and US bases across the Middle East.

A two-week ceasefire was agreed on April 8 through Pakistan's mediation, after Iran rejected earlier US proposals. The ceasefire has been violated by both sides since its declaration. The conflict has expanded to include fighting between Israel and Hezbollah in Lebanon, complicating peace efforts.
key_facts: War began with Israeli assassination of Iran's Supreme Leader on February 28
Current two-week US-Iran ceasefire mediated by Pakistan on April 8
Both sides have violated the ceasefire agreement
Conflict includes parallel Israel-Hezbollah war in Lebanon
sections.australia_relevance: Australia condemned the 8 April Lebanon attacks alongside 30+ other countries, reflecting concerns about regional escalation.

sections.label: Diplomacy
sections.title: Israel-Lebanon truce faces early violations amid wider talks
paragraphs: Israel and Lebanon agreed to a 10-day ceasefire on April 16, marking the first direct diplomatic engagement between the countries in decades. Lebanese forces reported several Israeli ceasefire violations within hours, including demolitions, artillery shelling and land-clearing operations in border areas.

Iran's parliamentary speaker said negotiations with the US are 'still far from a final agreement' despite progress on some issues. Trump suggested new US-Iran talks could happen 'over the next two days' in Pakistan, though Vice President JD Vance is expected to lead any second round of negotiations.
key_facts: Israel-Lebanon 10-day ceasefire began April 16 amid violation reports
First Israel-Lebanon direct talks in decades
US-Iran negotiations described as 'far from final agreement'
Pakistan continues mediating role in peace efforts

sections.label: Economy
sections.title: Australian fuel prices ease but supply concerns persist
paragraphs: Australian petrol prices fell by more than 5% to A$2.40 per litre in early April, the first decline since February but still about a third higher than early March levels. The federal government halved fuel excise for three months, reducing costs by 26.3 cents per litre.

Australia has not yet felt the full impact of oil supply shortages due to supply chain lags, with fuel deliveries assured until mid-April according to government officials. A fire this week at Victoria's Geelong Oil Refinery, Australia's largest producing 120,000 barrels daily, highlighted vulnerabilities in domestic refining capacity.
key_facts: Petrol prices fell 5% to A$2.40/litre but remain 33% above March levels
Government halved fuel excise for three months saving 26.3 cents/litre
Fire at major Geelong refinery highlights supply vulnerabilities
Physical fuel shortages have not yet reached Australia due to supply lags
sections.australia_relevance: Australia imports 90% of refined fuel, making it highly vulnerable to Middle East disruptions affecting global oil markets.

sections.label: Outlook
sections.title: Ceasefire extensions depend on Hormuz access and Lebanon stability
paragraphs: Iran briefly opened the Strait of Hormuz during the Lebanon ceasefire announcement but closed it again after the US refused to lift its naval blockade. Hezbollah has linked the Lebanon ceasefire to broader US-Iran diplomacy, with Iranian officials making clear that continued Israeli attacks in Lebanon would jeopardise peace negotiations.

US intelligence assessed roughly half of Iran's missile launchers remain intact after a month of fighting, with many potentially buried by strikes on underground tunnel entrances. The International Energy Agency presents scenarios assuming resumed Middle East oil deliveries by mid-year, though warns this may prove 'too optimistic'.
key_facts: Iran links Strait reopening to lifting of US naval blockade
Lebanon ceasefire success tied to broader US-Iran negotiations
About half of Iran's missile capacity reportedly still intact
Energy markets cautiously optimistic but risks remain high
sections.australia_relevance: Australian energy officials cancelled international climate talks to focus on domestic fuel supply management during the crisis.
